coredata.dc:title: Spatially confined synthesis of TiNb2O7 quantum dots onto mesoporous carbon and Ti3C2TX MXene for boosting lithium storage
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Daoguang
Tang, Cheng
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Xinlin
Zhu, Guanjia
Huang, Zhen-Dong
Du, Aijun
Zhang, Haijiao
coredata.dc:description: TiNb2O7 has been emerged as one of the most promising electrode materials for high-energy lithium-ion batteries. However, limited by the slow electron/ion transport kinetics, and insufficient active sites in the bulk structure, the TiNb2O7 electrode still suffers from unsatisfactory lithium storage performance. Herein, we demonstrate a spatially confined strategy toward a novel TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite through a triblock copolymer-directed one-pot solvothermal route, where TiNb2O7 quantum dots with a particle size of 2–3 nm are evenly embedded into N-doped mesoporous carbon (NMC) and Ti3C2TX MXene. Impressively, the as-prepared TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene anode exhibits a high reversible capacity (486.2 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles) and long cycle lifespan (363.4 mAh g−1 at ss1 A g−1 after 500 cycles). Both experimental and theorical results further demonstrate that such a superior lithium storage performance is mainly ascribed to the synergistic effect among 0D TiNb2O7 quantum dots, 2D Ti3C2TX MXene nanosheets, and N-doped mesoporous carbon. The strategy presented also opens up new horizon for space-confined preparation of high-performance electrode materials.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiNb2O7 quantum dots
Ti3C2TX MXene
Mesoporous carbon
Confined synthesis
Lithium-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of spatially confined synthesis of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene.
(a, b) SEM images, (c, d) TEM images and particle size distribution, (e, f) HRTEM images, (g) STEM image, and (h–l) elemental mapping images of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene.

(a) XRD patterns of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7. (b) Raman spectrum of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene. (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene and corresponding pore size distribution curve. (d) XPS survey spectrum of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, and high-resolution spectra of (e) Ti 2p, (f) Nb 3d, (g) O 1s, (h) C 1s, and (i) N 1s.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1, and (b) charge–discharge profiles of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode at 0.1 A g−1. (c) Cycling performances at 0.1 A g−1, (d) rate capabilities, and (e) capacity retention at different current densities of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes. (f) Comparison of rate performances of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode with other TiNb2O7-based electrodes.

(a) CV curves of the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode at different scan rates, (b) b-value analysis using the relationship between peak currents and scan rates, (c) capacitive contribution of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene at the scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1, (d) the percentages of capacitive contribution at different scan rates. (e) The
D
L i
+
calculated from the GITT curves of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene, and TiNb2O7, (f) electrochemical impedance spectra and the equivalent circuit diagram.

(a) The surface energy of (100), (010), (001), (110) and (111) facets of TiNb2O7 crystal, (b) adsorption energies of Li ion on TiNb2O7 (100) and (001), Ti3C2N and the interlayer of the heterostructures. The geometries of TiNb2O7 (100) surface (I), Ti3C2N (II) and (100)-Ti3C2N (III) with Li adsorption are presented. (c) Charge density difference of Li intercalated heterostructures (iso-value of 0.0015 e/Å3). The cyan and yellow areas represent the electron depletion and accumulation, respectively. The transferred charge between the Li ion and TiNb2O7 (Ti3C2N) surface has been displayed. (d) Schematic illustration of lithium storage advantages of the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode material.

head.ce:title: Spatially confined synthesis of TiNb2O7 quantum dots onto mesoporous carbon and Ti3C2TX MXene for boosting lithium storage

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors, Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, 9 Wenyuan Road, Nanjing, 210023, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: D. G. Sun and C. Tang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiNb2O7 has been emerged as one of the most promising electrode materials for high-energy lithium-ion batteries. However, limited by the slow electron/ion transport kinetics, and insufficient active sites in the bulk structure, the TiNb2O7 electrode still suffers from unsatisfactory lithium storage performance. Herein, we demonstrate a spatially confined strategy toward a novel TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite through a triblock copolymer-directed one-pot solvothermal route, where TiNb2O7 quantum dots with a particle size of 2–3 nm are evenly embedded into N-doped mesoporous carbon (NMC) and Ti3C2TX MXene. Impressively, the as-prepared TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene anode exhibits a high reversible capacity (486.2 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles) and long cycle lifespan (363.4 mAh g−1 at ss1 A g−1 after 500 cycles). Both experimental and theorical results further demonstrate that such a superior lithium storage performance is mainly ascribed to the synergistic effect among 0D TiNb2O7 quantum dots, 2D Ti3C2TX MXene nanosheets, and N-doped mesoporous carbon. The strategy presented also opens up new horizon for space-confined preparation of high-performance electrode materials.

A ternary TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite has been developed by a triblock copolymer-directed solvothermal assembly route, where ultrasmall TiNb2O7 quantum dots are spatially confined into N-doped mesoporous carbon and Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets. The resulting TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene anode shows an excellent electrochemical lithium storage capability.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are extensively employed in new energy tram, aerospace and intelligent wearable devices due to high energy density and long cycle life [1–4]. However, the explosive growth of energy storage equipment puts forward a higher demand for high-energy LIBs [5,6]. As the key component of LIBs, the structure and composition of electrode materials have a decisive effect on their energy density and cycling stability [7–9]. Regrettably, the commercial graphite with a relatively low theoretical capacity of 372 mAh g−1 is already difficult to meet the high energy density requirements [10–12]. Besides, the internal short circuit caused by the easy deposition of lithium dendrites also hinder its deep development in the field of fast-charging LIBs [13–15]. Therefore, developing anode materials with high capacity, long cycle life and excellent rate performance is an urgent problem need to be solved in LIBs [16–18].

The researchers found that niobium-based materials including TiNb2O7, Ti2Nb10O29, and Nb2O5 have the unique advantages in the rapid lithium-ion storage. In 2011, Goodenough's team first reported the monoclinic structure of TiNb2O7 as anodes for LIBs, which exhibited a superior cycling performance [19]. In recent years, TiNb2O7 has aroused more and more attention in LIBs owing to high theoretical specific capacity, superior structural stability, and safe voltage platform. To better improve the electrochemical properties of TiNb2O7, various strategies such as heteroatom doping, carbon coating, and porous engineering have been developed in the last ten years [20,21]. For example, to improve the rate capability of TiNb2O7, Lee et al. synthesized ordered mesoporous TiNb2O7 by the block copolymer-assisted self-assembly method. The mesoporous structure can effectively shorten the diffusion distance of Li ions/electrons and promote the rapid penetration of the electrolyte, thus obtaining an excellent rate performance [22]. Besides, Zhang et al. prepared hierarchically porous TiNb2O7 microspheres through a solvothermal method [23]. The constructed hierarchical structure endowed TiNb2O7 microspheres with excellent rate performance and long cycle life when used as the LIBs anode material. Despite some progress has been made, the low electronic conductivity of TiNb2O7 itself still restricts its further application. As a countermeasure, Sun et al. designed carbon-coated mesoporous TiNb2O7 microspheres by a simple solvothermal reaction, and the prepared TiNb2O7/C composite showed an excellent lithium storage performance with a high rate performance (200 mAh g−1 at 30 C) and long cycle performance (191 mAh g−1 at 10 C after 500 cycles) [24]. Although the lithium storage performance of TiNb2O7 has been improved based on the rational design of materials, the TiNb2O7 electrodes still exhibit unsatisfactory lithium-ion storage performance due to slow electron/ion transport dynamics, and few active sites in the bulk structure. Consequently, it is necessary to further optimize the structure and composition for better enhancing the electrochemical performance of TiNb2O7.

In this work, we present a ternary TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite by a scalable triblock copolymer-directed solvothermal assembly rousste. In the framework, N-doped mesoporous carbon (NMC) is uniformly arranged onto the surface of two-dimensional (2D) Ti3C2Tx MXene, which can greatly shorten the ion transport path and buffer the volume expansion during deep cycles. Most importantly, ultrasmall TiNb2O7 quantum dots are well confined between mesoporous carbon and MXene, which can effectively increase the contact area between electrolyte and the electrode material, and strengthen the structural stability of the whole electrode. As a result, the resulting TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite shows an excellent rate performance and satisfactory cycle stability when served as the LIBs anode. Density functional theory calculation further demonstrates the synergistically enhanced lithium storage mechanism between 0D TiNb2O7 nanoparticles and 2D MXene nanosheets.

Triblock copolymer Pluronic P123 (PEO20PPO70PEO20, Mw = 5800) was provided by Sigma–Aldrich. Titanium aluminum carbide (Ti3AlC2), lithium fluoride (LiF), dopamine hydrochloride (C8H11NO2·HCl), niobium(V) chloride (NbCl5) and tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) were purchased from Chinese Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.

Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets were prepared according to our previous work [25]. Typically, 1 g of LiF was added into 20 mL of 9 mol L−1 HCl solution. After stirring for 5 min, 1 g of Ti3C2Tx powder was slowly added to the above solution, and continuously stirred at 40 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the obtained precipitate was repeatedly washed with deionized water until pH > 6. The collected precipitate was re-dispersed into 60 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 1 h, and centrifuged at 3500 r min−1 for 1 h to harvest the supernatant.

In a typical procedure, 200 mg of P123 was dispersed into 40 mL of ethanol and vigorously stirred for 1 h. Then, 20 mg of the Ti3C2Tx MXene power was added to the above solution and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. Subsequently, 100 mg of NbCl5, 20 mg of TBOT and 30 mg of C8H11NO2·HCl were further added in the above mixture and kept stirring for another 2 h. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and treated at 180 °C for 24 h. The collected product was washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol, then dried at 60 °C in vacuum for 12 h. The harvested powder was heated to 600 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 to get the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene. Meanwhile, the TiNb2O7/MXene composite was prepared according to the above procedures without the addition of C8H11NO2·HCl.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were obtained from the Japan Rigakul D/max-2550 instrument using CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). The morphologies and structure of the samples were observed on scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8200, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 200CX, Japan) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). Raman spectroscopy was measured using laser excitation at 785 nm on the inVia Reflection Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured by using a micromeritics ASAP 2010 analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha.

To evaluate the electrochemical performances, active material, carboxymethyl cellulose, and Super P were mixed with a weight ratio of 80:10:10 in deionized water to shape uniform slurries, which were homogeneously covered on the Cu foil, and then dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h used as a working electrode. After that, the Cu foil was cut into disc with the diameters of 12 mm, and the active materials on the copper foil was about 0.8–1.0 mg cm−2. The CR-2032 coin half-cell were assembled in argon-filled glove box with lithium metal as counter electrode, 2400 microporous polypropylene film as separator, 1.0 mol L−1 LPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate and propylene carbonate (EC/PC; 1:1 by volume) as the electrolyte. Galvanostatic charge–discharge properties were tested on LAND CT2001A battery tester at different current densities between 0.005 V and 3.00 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were performed on the CHI–660C analyzer at different scan rates, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured over a frequency range of 100 kHz-0.01 Hz.

All calculations were implemented in the Vienna ab-initio simulation package (VASP) on the basis of the density functional theory (DFT) [26]. The Perdew-Burke-Emzerhof functional of generalized gradient approximation (GGA-PBE) was adopted to describe the exchange-correlation energy [27]. The cut-off energy was set to be 400 eV, while the 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst k-mesh was used for the large supercell. To screen the periodic interlayer interaction along the z direction, a vacuum layer of more than 15 Å is adopted in all calculations. Besides, the van der Waals interaction is corrected by the DFT-D3 method. The convergent criterions were set to be 10−5 eV and 0.01 eV Å−1 for the energy and force on each atom, respectively. The surface formation energy (E
F ) of non-van der Waals layered TiNb2O7 crystal can be expressed as
E F
=
E
s u r f
− N ε
2 A
where E
surf is the energy of the surface model, N is the number of atoms in the cell, A is the area of the surface. ϵ represents the cohesive energy (E
coh ) per atom of the TiNb2O7 bulk, which can be calculated by
E
c o h
=
(
x μ
T i
+
2 x μ
N b
+ 7 x
μ O
− E
b u l k
)
/ 10 x

here, x is the number of the unit cell in the supercell, and E
bulk represents the total energy of the TiNb2O7 crystal. μ
Ti , μ
Nb and μ
O is the chemical potential of Ti, Nb and O atoms, respectively. This is obtained by the average energy of the most stable bulk phase of the metal elements and the oxygen gas. Moreover, the adsorption energy (E
ads ) of Li ion is calculated by
E
a d s
=
E
S u r f − L i
−
E
s u r f
−
μ
L i
where E
surf-Li is the total energy for the surface after Li adsorption, and μ
Li is the chemical potential of Li atom.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the typical preparation process of the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite. Firstly, Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets were prepared by chemical etching method, which shows an ultrathin 2D structure with relatively smooth surface (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, triblock copolymer P123 was fully dissolved in ethanol to form a uniform transparent solution. Then, MXene nanosheets and dopamine hydrochloride were added to P123 solution under stirring. Under the drive force of hydrogen bonding, P123 micelles and dopamine hydrochloride were effectively assembled onto the MXene nanosheets to form the uniform mesoporous structure. After that, TBOT and NbCl5 were added to the above solution and Ti+4/Nb+5 was adsorbed onto the surface of MXene nanosheets via electrostatic action. In the subsequent hydrothermal process, ultrasmall TiNb2O7 nanoparticles were successfully embedded between the mesoporous carbon layer and MXene.

The morphology of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene was firstly investigated by SEM images. It can be clearly seen from the low-magnification SEM image that the obtained TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene presents a uniform morphology and distinct sheet structure (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, these nanosheets are much thicker than MXene, suggesting the growth of TiNb2O7 particles and carbon layer. The magnified SEM image further reveals the existence of small nanoparticles on the surface (Fig. 2b). Then, TEM technique was used to further observe the microstructure of the composite. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, a lot of ultrasmall nanoparticles with a size of 2∼3 nm based on the software Image J analysis by selecting dozens of particles, and mesopores are closely patterned onto the MXene nanosheets, as marked by the yellow dot circle and orange framework. HRTEM image further demonstrates the mesoporous structure (Fig. 2e), and the lattice spacing of the (110) crystal plane is measured to be 0.37 nm (Fig. 2f). In the case, the formation of ultrasmall TiNb2O7 quantum dots is caused by the spatially confined effect of mesoporous carbon, as well as the MXene substrate. Besides, the TiNb2O7/MXene product was also prepared in absence of dopamine hydrochloride. As shown in Fig. S2, the obtained TiNb2O7 particles displays the irregular morphology with an average particle size of 40 nm and randomly distributes onto the surface of MXene, verifying the confined effect of mesoporous carbon. Meanwhile, pure TiNb2O7 product presents irregular clumps (Fig. S3). On the other hand, EDX element mapping (Fig. 2g–l) shows the uniform distribution of Nb, Ti, O, C and N elements in the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene.

The crystal structure of samples was revealed by the XRD analysis. Fig. 3 a clearly shows that the diffraction peaks of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene are well matched with the standard XRD pattern (JCPDS No.77-1374) of TiNb2O7 [28]. No obviously characteristic peaks from mesoporous carbon are detected, illustrating its amorphous structure. Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectrum of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene. Two peaks at 1355 cm−1 and 1603 cm−1 are clearly presented, which can be ascribed to D- and G-bands of carbon materials. The intensity ratio of two peaks (ID/IG) are calculated to 0.97, indicating the relatively higher disorder degree of carbon layer [29]. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured to analyze the specific surface area and pore structure of three samples including TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7. As displayed in Fig. 3c and S4, TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene has the highest Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of 107.5 m2 g−1, which is obviously higher than that of TiNb2O7/MXene (67.8 m2 g−1) and TiNb2O7 (25.7 m2 g−1). And the average pore diameter for TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene is about 4.0 nm according to the pore size distribution curve (inset of Fig. 3c). The large specific surface area and narrow mesopore distribution are conducive to the contact between the electrode and electrolyte, thus promoting the rapid lithium ion transport. XPS was employed to then analyze the surface chemical state information of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene. The survey scanning spectrum clearly proves five signal peaks (Fig. 3d), corresponding to Ti 2p, Nb 3d, O 1s, C 1s and N 1s, respectively. In contrast, only the signal peaks of Ti 2p, Nb 3d, O 1s and C 1s are demonstrated for TiNb2O7/MXene (Fig. S5), verifying that the N atoms in TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene are due to the introduction of dopamine. Fig. 3e displays the high-resolution Ti 2p spectrum, two peaks at 455.3 eV and 461.2 eV are attributed to the Ti–C bond, and the peak at 464.8 eV is ascribed to Ti–O bond [30–32]. As can be seen from Fig. 3f, the high-resolution spectra of Nb 3d exhibits two strong peaks at 210.0 eV and 207.1 eV, which are assigned to Nb 3d3/2 and Nb 3d5/2, respectively [28,33]. In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3g), the peak at 529.6 eV is attributed to oxygen element in TiNb2O7, while the weak peak at 531.9 eV is derived from the adsorbed water [34]. The C 1s spectrum exhibits three characteristic peaks at 284.5, 286.2 eV and 288.0 eV (Fig. 3h), which can be attributed to the C–C, C–O, and C=O bonds, respectively [35,36]. High-resolution N 1s spectra is shown in Fig. 3i. The broad peak of N 1s can be resolved into three peaks at 398.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 401.8 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N [36,37], indicating the successful in-situ nitrogen doping, which can further improve the electrical conductivity of the electrode material. Besides, TiNb2O7/MXene shows the similar high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p, Nb 3d, O 1s and C 1s (Fig. S6), indicating that the introduction of dopamine has no obvious effect on the chemical states of TiNb2O7. The above XPS results further verify the successful construction of ternary TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite.

The electrochemical performance of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes were tested as anodes for LIBs by assembling half-cells. Fig. 4 a shows the CV curves of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode at sweep rate of 0.1 mV s−1. In the first cathode scan, the peak of about 0.8 V is related to the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film, while the peaks at 1.65 V and 1.78 V correspond to the reduction of Nb5+ to Nb4+. The following CV curves are almost overlapped, demonstrating a highly reversibility. Fig. 4b shows the charge–discharge curves of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode in the first three cycles at the current density of 0.1 A g−1. The initial discharge and charge capacities are 883.6 mAh g−1 and 597.0 mAh g−1, corresponding to the initial coulomb efficiency (ICE) of 67.5%. The irreversible capacity loss is mainly due to the formation of solid electrolyte interface films and electrolyte decomposition. The cycling performances of different electrodes were investigated at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 as presented in Fig. 4c. Compared with TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes, the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode exhibits the highest reversible capacity of 486.2 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles, and the capacity retention rate is up to 85.5%. Furthermore, the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode still retains the 2D structure after cycling (Fig. S7), and no serious cracking or pulverization can be seen, showing a good structural stability. The enhanced lithium storage performance of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode benefits from the more energy storage active sites and faster lithium ion transport dynamics provided by the unique structural design. Fig. 4d shows the rate capabilities of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes at different current density. Obviously, the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode delivers the highest specific capacities at each current density compared to TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes. At current density of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1, the reversible capacities are 505.4, 428.5, 372.3, 324.0 and 272.8 mAh g−1, respectively. The capacity retention rates of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode under 0.2, 0.5 and 1.0 A g−1 current densities are 84.2%, 75% and 68.9%, respectively. Even when the current density increased to 2.0 A g−1, the capacity retention rate remains at 68.3% (Fig. 4e). More importantly, the rate performance of the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode is superior to than most previously reported TiNb2O7-based electrodes (Fig. 4f and Table S1) [38–47], further demonstrates the unique advantages of the design strategy proposed in this work for improving the lithium storage performance of TiNb2O7. Besides, the cycling performances at the high current density of 1 A g−1 was also evaluated. Seen from Fig. S8, the reversible capacity still maintains 363.4 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles for TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode, while for TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes, only 354.2 mAh g−1 and 172.3 mAh g−1 are retained after 500 cycles, respectively, further indicates the good cyclic stability of TiNb2O7/MXene electrode.

To understand the electrochemical kinetics of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode, CV test were carried out at scanning rate of 0.2–2.0 mV s−1 (Fig. 5 a). The degree of capacitance effect can be estimated from the relationship between peak current (
i
) and scan rate (
v
) according to the following formulas [48,49]:
(1)
i = a
v b
(2)
log
( i )
= b log
( v )
+ log
( a )
where a and b are adjustable values. The b value is determined from the slope of the
log
( i )
versus
log
( v )
plot. When b-value is 0.5, the diffusion process controls the electrochemical system. As b value is close to 1.0, the capacitive process dominates the charge–discharge process. As shown in Fig. 5b, the b value is calculated to 0.87 and 0.94 for cathodic and anodic peaks, respectively. This result indicates that the electrochemical kinetics is mainly dominated by capacitive behavior. The contribution of the capacitive behavior (
k 1
v
) and diffusion control (
k 2
v
1 2
) at different scan rates can be assessed quantitatively from the following equation [50]:
(3)
i
( v )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 2

According to Fig. 5c, the capacitive contribution rate of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene is 78.1% at scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1. Besides, the capacitive contribution ratios at different scan rates were also calculated. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 5d that the capacitance contribution rates are gradually increased with the increase of scan rates. Even at a low scan speed of 0.2 mV s−1, the capacitive contribution rate is 62%. The higher rate of capacitive contribution is conducive to improving the rate performance, which is consistent with the above experimental results. To further reveal the dynamic behavior of different electrodes, the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) is used to evaluate the Li+ ion diffusion coefficient
(
D
L i
+
)
. The GITT curves of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene, TiNb2O7/MXene and TiNb2O7 electrodes are shown in Fig. S9, and the Li+ diffusion coefficient can be calculated using the following equation [51]:
(4)
D =
4
π τ
(
n m
V m
S
)
2
(
Δ E s
Δ
E τ
)
2
where τ represents the time duration of pulse, S is the electrode/electrolyte contact area, n
m is molar mass, V
m is volume of electrode material, and ΔEs and ΔEτ are the voltage change before and after the current pulse, and during the current pulse, respectively. Fig. 5e clearly shows that the D
Li + , value of TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene (1.3 × 10−10 to 9.4 × 10−10 cm2 s−1) is larger higher than that of TiNb2O7/MXene (1.2 × 10−10 to 5.1 × 10−10 cm2 s−1) and TiNb2O7 (0.8 × 10−10 to 2.9 × 10−10 cm2 s−1). It is demonstrated that the unique structural design can promote the rapid transport of lithium ions. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to probe the charge transfer kinetics of the different electrodes. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene electrode exhibits the lowest charge-transfer resistance (Rct ) of 24.6 Ω, which is obviously smaller than that of TiNb2O7/MXene (55.7 Ω) and TiNb2O7 (68.1 Ω). This result indicates that N-doped mesoporous carbon can effectively promote charge transport kinetics.

To unveil the origin of the improved Li storage performance on the TiNb2O7-MXene heterostructure, we perform the first-principle calculations and focus on the geometric and electronic behaviour of Li adsorption. Firstly, the formation energy of different surfaces on TiNb2O7 crystal, including (100), (010), (001), (110) and (111) facets, are calculated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the (100) and (001) facets are more energetically favourite in comparison to other facets since they have more negative surface energy (−2.41 eV and −2.15 eV for (100) and (001) facets, respectively). Therefore, we mainly focus on these two facets in the following analysis. Moreover, Li ion prefers to adsorb on the exposed O and N atoms on the TiNb2O7 surface and N saturated MXene, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6b. The Li adsorption energy reaches −3.67 and −3.25 eV for (100) and (001) facets, respectively, and it is slightly larger than that of Ti3C2N (−2.97 eV). In the TiNb2O7-MXene heterostructure [(100)-Ti3C2N and (001)-Ti3C2N], the Li adsorption energy further enhances due to the strong bonding between both TiNb2O7 and Ti3C2N surfaces. It indicates the improved performance on Li ion storage. We then evaluate the charge transfer after Li intercalation in the interlayer of heterostructures through the charge density difference and bader analysis (Fig. 6c). Clearly, the electron is mainly depleted from the Li ion and accumulated around the surface of TiNb2O7 and Ti3C2N surfaces, indicating strong electron transfer from Li to the exposed O and N atoms. The bader analysis shows that 0.66e and 0.63e electrons depletes from the Li ion in (100)- and (001)-Ti3C2N heterostructures. The transferred electron is significantly enhanced in heterostructure after Li adsorption compared with that in bare (100) and (001) TiNb2O7 (0.56e and 0.49e) and Ti3C2N (0.37e) surface, indicating the synergistic effect between the TiNb2O7 and Ti3C2N surface for Li storage. As a result, the TiNb2O7-MXene heterostructure is an ideal electrode material to achieve the enhanced Li storage performance.

According to the above results, it can be concluded that the excellent electrochemical properties of ternary TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite are closely related to their unique structure and composition (Fig. 6d). On one hand, mesoporous structure effectively enhances the transport kinetics of lithium ions, and the doping of nitrogen in the carbon layer improves the electrical conductivity of the whole electrode material. On the other hand, ultra-small TiNb2O7 quantum dots are well embedded between MXene and mesoporous carbon, which greatly increases the structural stability and contact area between electrolyte and the electrode. Accordingly, the perfect combination of the above merits endows the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite with superior lithium storage performance.

In short, a facile space-confined strategy has been developed for obtaining the ternary TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite by a scalable copolymer-guided one-pot solvothermal process. In this newly constructed framework, ultrasmall TiNb2O7 quantum dots with a particle size of 2–3 nm are evenly embedded into nitrogen-doped mesoporous carbon and 2D Ti3C2TX nanosheets. With the merits in the composition and structure, the TiNb2O7-NMC/MXene composite anode shows an excellent electrochemical lithium-ion storage capability with a high reversible capacity of 486.2 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles. Furthermore, even when the current density increases to 1 A g−1, the reversible capacity is still as high as 363.4 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles, showing the superior cycling stability. Meanwhile, this work provides a new clue for the development of high-performance multicomponent electrode materials.

Daoguang Sun: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Cheng Tang: Software. Haitao Li: Investigation. Xinlin Zhang: Formal analysis. Guanjia Zhu: Validation. Zhen-Dong Huang: Writing – review & editing. Aijun Du: Software. Haijiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.
